name: Metformin
description: Metformin is a first-line medication for type 2 diabetes that works by decreasing glucose production in the liver and improving your body's sensitivity to insulin. It is one of the most widely prescribed diabetes medications worldwide and has been used safely for decades.
uses: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (as monotherapy or in combination with other medications). Sometimes used off-label for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), weight management, and prediabetes prevention.
dosage: Adults: Usually started at 500 mg twice daily or 850 mg once daily with meals. May be increased gradually to a maximum of 2,550 mg/day in divided doses. Extended-release: 500–2,000 mg once daily with evening meal. Always take with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach pain, loss of appetite, metallic taste. These often improve after a few weeks. Serious (rare): Lactic acidosis (seek emergency help if you experience unusual muscle pain, difficulty breathing, unusual tiredness, or stomach pain with vomiting).
warnings: Do not use if you have severe kidney disease, metabolic acidosis, or diabetic ketoacidosis. Temporarily discontinue before contrast dye procedures. May cause vitamin B12 deficiency with long-term use — ask your doctor about monitoring. Avoid excessive alcohol use.
interactions: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (topiramate, zonisamide) may increase lactic acidosis risk. Contrast dyes used in imaging procedures require temporary discontinuation. Alcohol increases lactic acidosis risk. Some drugs affecting kidney function may require dose adjustment.
pregnancy: Category B — Generally considered safe in pregnancy, though insulin is typically preferred for gestational diabetes. Discuss with your healthcare provider.
storage: Store at room temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C). Keep away from moisture and heat.

name: Lisinopril
description: Lisinopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, and to improve survival after heart attacks. It works by relaxing blood vessels, making it easier for the heart to pump blood.
uses: Hypertension (high blood pressure), heart failure, acute myocardial infarction (post-heart attack), diabetic nephropathy (kidney protection in diabetics).
dosage: Hypertension: Initially 10 mg once daily, maintenance 20–40 mg once daily. Heart failure: Initially 2.5–5 mg once daily, target dose 20–40 mg daily. Can be taken with or without food, at the same time each day.
sideEffects: Common: Dry cough (occurs in up to 10% of patients), dizziness, headache, fatigue. Serious: Angioedema (swelling of face, lips, tongue, throat — seek emergency care immediately), hyperkalemia (high potassium), kidney function changes, low blood pressure.
warnings: Do NOT use during pregnancy — can cause serious harm or death to an unborn baby. Do not use with aliskiren in patients with diabetes. May cause a persistent dry cough; if bothersome, your doctor may switch to an ARB. Monitor kidney function and potassium levels regularly.
interactions: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) may reduce effectiveness and increase kidney risk. Potassium supplements and potassium-sparing diuretics may cause dangerously high potassium. Lithium levels may be increased. Do not combine with ARBs or aliskiren in diabetics.
pregnancy: Category D — Contraindicated in pregnancy. Can cause fetal injury and death, particularly in the second and third trimesters. Discontinue as soon as pregnancy is detected.
storage: Store at room temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C). Protect from moisture.

name: Atorvastatin
description: Atorvastatin is a statin medication that lowers cholesterol and triglycerides in the blood. It is one of the most prescribed medications worldwide and has been shown to significantly reduce the risk of heart attacks and strokes.
uses: Primary hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, prevention of cardiovascular disease in high-risk patients, familial hypercholesterolemia. Used alongside diet and exercise modifications.
dosage: Adults: 10–80 mg once daily. Starting dose depends on LDL cholesterol level and cardiovascular risk. Can be taken at any time of day, with or without food. Do not break or crush tablets.
sideEffects: Common: Muscle pain or weakness, joint pain, diarrhea, nausea, headache, insomnia. Serious (rare): Rhabdomyolysis (severe muscle breakdown — seek emergency help for unexplained muscle pain with dark urine), liver damage (report persistent nausea, upper stomach pain, dark urine, jaundice).
warnings: Report unexplained muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness immediately. Liver function should be tested before starting and as clinically indicated. Avoid grapefruit juice in large amounts. Do not use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
interactions: Grapefruit juice may increase drug levels. Certain antibiotics (clarithromycin, erythromycin) and antifungals (itraconazole) increase risk of muscle damage. Cyclosporine, gemfibrozil, and niacin increase rhabdomyolysis risk. Warfarin effects may be enhanced.
pregnancy: Category X — Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Cholesterol is needed for fetal development. Women of childbearing age should use effective contraception.
storage: Store at room temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C). Keep in original container.

name: Omeprazole
description: Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor that reduces the amount of acid produced in the stomach. It is used to treat conditions caused by excess stomach acid, including gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), stomach ulcers, and Zollinger-Ellison syndrome.
uses: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), erosive esophagitis, gastric and duodenal ulcers, Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, H. pylori eradication (in combination with antibiotics), prevention of NSAID-associated ulcers.
dosage: GERD: 20 mg once daily for 4–8 weeks. Erosive esophagitis: 20 mg once daily for 4–8 weeks. Ulcers: 20–40 mg once daily. Take 30–60 minutes before a meal, preferably in the morning. Swallow capsules whole; do not crush or chew.
sideEffects: Common: Headache, stomach pain, nausea, diarrhea, gas, constipation. Long-term use: Increased risk of bone fractures (hip, wrist, spine), vitamin B12 deficiency, magnesium deficiency, C. difficile infection. Rare: Kidney inflammation.
warnings: Long-term use (>1 year) may increase fracture risk and nutrient deficiencies. Not intended for immediate heartburn relief. May mask symptoms of gastric cancer. Discuss with your doctor if you need to use for more than 14 days (OTC) or beyond prescribed duration.
interactions: Reduces absorption of drugs requiring stomach acid: ketoconazole, iron supplements, erlotinib. May increase methotrexate levels. Reduces clopidogrel (Plavix) effectiveness — avoid combination if possible. May affect some HIV medications.
pregnancy: Category C — Use only if clearly needed. Limited human data; animal studies show some risk at high doses. Discuss benefits vs. risks with your healthcare provider.
storage: Store at room temperature (59–86°F / 15–30°C). Keep container tightly closed and away from light and moisture.

name: Sertraline
description: Sertraline is an antidepressant belonging to the SSRI class. It works by increasing serotonin levels in the brain, helping to improve mood, anxiety, and related symptoms. It is one of the most commonly prescribed antidepressants and has a well-established safety profile.
uses: Major depressive disorder, panic disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), social anxiety disorder, premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD).
dosage: Depression/OCD: Start at 50 mg once daily, may increase by 25–50 mg at weekly intervals. Maximum 200 mg/day. Panic/PTSD/Social anxiety: Start at 25 mg once daily, increase to 50 mg after one week. Take in the morning or evening consistently. May be taken with or without food.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea, diarrhea, insomnia, drowsiness, dizziness, dry mouth, sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, delayed ejaculation), sweating, tremor. Most side effects improve within 1–2 weeks. Serious: Serotonin syndrome (rare, with other serotonergic drugs), increased suicidal thoughts in young adults (monitor closely).
warnings: FDA Black Box Warning: Antidepressants may increase suicidal thinking and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults (ages 18–24). Monitor closely, especially during the first few months. Do not stop abruptly — taper gradually to avoid discontinuation symptoms. Do not combine with MAO inhibitors (wait at least 14 days).
interactions: MAO inhibitors (dangerous combination — do not use within 14 days). Other serotonergic drugs (triptans, tramadol, St. John's Wort) increase serotonin syndrome risk. Warfarin and NSAIDs increase bleeding risk. Pimozide is contraindicated. Alcohol should be avoided.
pregnancy: Category C — Use during third trimester may cause neonatal adaptation syndrome. Some studies suggest a small increased risk of persistent pulmonary hypertension in newborns. Discuss risks and benefits with your doctor; untreated depression also carries risks during pregnancy.
storage: Store at room temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C). Keep tightly closed.

name: Amoxicillin
description: Amoxicillin is a widely used penicillin-type antibiotic that fights bacteria in the body. It is effective against a broad range of bacterial infections and is one of the most commonly prescribed antibiotics, especially for children.
uses: Ear infections (otitis media), strep throat, pneumonia, bronchitis, urinary tract infections, skin infections, H. pylori eradication (with other medications), dental infections, sinusitis.
dosage: Adults: 250–500 mg every 8 hours, or 500–875 mg every 12 hours depending on infection severity. Children: 25–45 mg/kg/day in divided doses. Duration typically 7–14 days; complete the full course even if you feel better. May be taken with or without food.
sideEffects: Common: Diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, skin rash, headache. Serious: Allergic reactions (hives, difficulty breathing, swelling of face/throat — seek emergency help), C. difficile-associated diarrhea (persistent watery diarrhea), severe skin reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome — very rare).
warnings: Do not use if you have a known penicillin allergy. Tell your doctor about any previous allergic reactions to antibiotics. May cause a non-allergic rash in patients with mononucleosis (Epstein-Barr virus). Complete the full prescribed course to prevent antibiotic resistance.
interactions: Probenecid increases amoxicillin levels. May reduce effectiveness of oral contraceptives (use backup contraception). Methotrexate levels may be increased. Allopurinol increases rash risk. Warfarin effects may be enhanced — monitor INR.
pregnancy: Category B — Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Widely used for treating infections during pregnancy. Crosses the placenta but no evidence of harm in human studies.
storage: Capsules/tablets: Store at room temperature. Liquid suspension: Refrigerate after reconstitution, discard after 14 days. Keep all forms away from moisture.

name: Ibuprofen
description: Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used to reduce fever and treat pain and inflammation. It works by blocking the production of prostaglandins, substances that contribute to inflammation, pain, and fever.
uses: Mild to moderate pain (headache, toothache, menstrual cramps, muscle aches), fever reduction, arthritis (osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis), minor injuries, back pain. Available both over-the-counter and in higher prescription strengths.
dosage: OTC: 200–400 mg every 4–6 hours as needed (max 1,200 mg/day). Prescription: Up to 800 mg three times daily (max 3,200 mg/day). Take with food or milk to reduce stomach irritation. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.
sideEffects: Common: Stomach pain, nausea, heartburn, dizziness, headache, rash. Serious: GI bleeding or ulceration (especially in older adults), cardiovascular events (heart attack, stroke — risk increases with long-term use), kidney damage, liver problems, severe allergic reactions.
warnings: NSAIDs increase the risk of serious cardiovascular events including heart attack and stroke. Risk increases with duration of use and in patients with heart disease. Can cause serious GI adverse events including bleeding, ulceration, and perforation. Avoid in the third trimester of pregnancy. Not recommended for patients about to undergo coronary artery bypass surgery.
interactions: Aspirin (ibuprofen may interfere with aspirin's cardioprotective effect — take aspirin at least 30 minutes before). ACE inhibitors and ARBs — reduced blood pressure effect, increased kidney risk. Warfarin and other blood thinners — increased bleeding risk. Lithium levels may be increased. Other NSAIDs — do not combine.
pregnancy: Category C (first and second trimester), Category D (third trimester). Avoid after 30 weeks of pregnancy — may cause premature closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus. Use only if clearly needed in early pregnancy.
storage: Store at room temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C). Keep container tightly closed.

name: Levothyroxine
description: Levothyroxine is a synthetic form of the thyroid hormone thyroxine (T4). It is used to treat hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid) by replacing the hormone that your thyroid gland cannot produce in sufficient quantities.
uses: Hypothyroidism, thyroid hormone replacement after thyroidectomy, TSH suppression in thyroid cancer management, myxedema coma (IV formulation).
dosage: Adults: Typical starting dose 25–50 mcg daily, increased by 12.5–25 mcg every 4–6 weeks based on TSH levels. Full replacement dose averages 1.6 mcg/kg/day. Take on an empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before breakfast, with water only. Consistent daily timing is important.
sideEffects: When properly dosed, side effects are uncommon (symptoms usually indicate over-replacement): Rapid heartbeat, palpitations, tremor, anxiety, insomnia, sweating, weight loss, diarrhea, headache. Under-dosing symptoms: Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin.
warnings: Not for weight loss treatment — can cause serious or life-threatening effects at high doses. Dose adjustments may be needed during pregnancy. Requires regular TSH monitoring (every 6–8 weeks after dose changes, then every 6–12 months). May take 4–6 weeks to feel full effects of a dose change.
interactions: Calcium supplements, iron, antacids, and certain foods reduce absorption — separate by at least 4 hours. Cholestyramine and sucralfate reduce absorption. Warfarin effects may be enhanced. Estrogen therapy may increase dose requirements. Coffee and fiber can reduce absorption.
pregnancy: Category A — Safe and important to continue during pregnancy. Thyroid hormone requirements typically increase 25–50% during pregnancy. TSH should be monitored every 4 weeks during the first half of pregnancy.
storage: Store at room temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C). Protect from light and moisture. Keep in original container.

name: Semaglutide
description: Semaglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist used for type 2 diabetes and chronic weight management. It mimics a natural gut hormone that helps regulate blood sugar, appetite, and digestion. It has gained significant attention for its effectiveness in weight loss.
uses: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (Ozempic, Rybelsus), chronic weight management in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related conditions (Wegovy). Has also shown cardiovascular risk reduction benefits in the SELECT trial.
dosage: Ozempic (injection): Start 0.25 mg weekly for 4 weeks, then 0.5 mg weekly; may increase to 1 mg then 2 mg weekly. Wegovy (injection): Gradual escalation from 0.25 mg to 2.4 mg weekly over 16–20 weeks. Rybelsus (oral): Start 3 mg daily for 30 days, then 7 mg, may increase to 14 mg. Take oral form on empty stomach with small sip of water, 30 min before food.
sideEffects: Common: Nausea (most common, especially during dose escalation), vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, decreased appetite. These typically improve over weeks. Serious (rare): Pancreatitis, gallbladder problems, kidney injury from dehydration due to vomiting/diarrhea, possible thyroid tumors (seen in animal studies).
warnings: FDA Black Box Warning: Thyroid C-cell tumors seen in animal studies — contraindicated in patients with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2). Monitor for pancreatitis symptoms. Risk of hypoglycemia when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas. May affect absorption of oral medications due to delayed gastric emptying.
interactions: Insulin and sulfonylureas — increased hypoglycemia risk, dose reduction may be needed. Oral medications — delayed gastric emptying may affect absorption timing. Warfarin — monitor INR more frequently when starting. Oral contraceptives — consider switching to non-oral contraception or backup method.
pregnancy: Not recommended — discontinue at least 2 months before planned pregnancy due to long half-life. Limited human data. Animal studies showed adverse effects. Women of reproductive potential should use effective contraception.
storage: Injection pens: Refrigerate (36–46°F / 2–8°C) before first use. After first use, can be kept at room temperature or refrigerated for up to 56 days. Do not freeze. Protect from light. Oral tablets: Store at room temperature.

name: Diphenhydramine
description: Diphenhydramine is a first-generation antihistamine used to relieve allergy symptoms, treat motion sickness, and as a short-term sleep aid. It works by blocking the effects of histamine, a substance produced by the body during allergic reactions.
uses: Allergic rhinitis (hay fever), urticaria (hives), itching, common cold symptoms, motion sickness, short-term insomnia, mild allergic reactions, adjunct in anaphylaxis treatment.
dosage: Adults: 25–50 mg every 4–6 hours as needed (max 300 mg/day). As a sleep aid: 50 mg at bedtime. Children 6–12: 12.5–25 mg every 4–6 hours. Not recommended for children under 6 without medical advice. Can be taken with or without food.
sideEffects: Common: Drowsiness (significant), dry mouth, dizziness, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention. In older adults: Increased risk of confusion, falls, cognitive impairment. Paradoxical excitation may occur in children. Tolerance to sedative effects develops quickly.
warnings: Causes significant drowsiness — do not drive or operate machinery. Not recommended as a long-term sleep aid. Use with caution in older adults (listed on Beers Criteria for potentially inappropriate medications in the elderly). May worsen glaucoma, urinary retention, and enlarged prostate symptoms. Avoid alcohol.
interactions: CNS depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids) — enhanced sedation. MAO inhibitors — increased anticholinergic effects. Other anticholinergic drugs — cumulative side effects. May mask symptoms of ototoxicity from other medications.
pregnancy: Category B — Generally considered safe for occasional use during pregnancy. Commonly recommended by OB/GYNs for pregnancy-related nausea and insomnia. Avoid near delivery as it may cause irritability in newborns.
storage: Store at room temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C). Protect from moisture. Liquid formulations should not be frozen.
